title: Outfit Your Shop
summary: Build the things that make everything else easier — in the order that compounds.
description:
Every project on this path makes the next one better. That is the whole idea.

The sequence is not by difficulty. It is by LEVERAGE: the crosscut sled comes first even though it is not the easiest build here, because from the moment it exists every crosscut you make for the rest of your life is square. Building it fourth would mean building the first three badly.

This is also the cheapest path on the site. Shop furniture is made from plywood and construction lumber, nobody cares what it looks like, and the mistakes are invisible inside a cabinet. It is the ideal place to practise on someone else's dime — your own.

steps.reason: First, and not because it is easy. A table saw without a sled cuts approximately square; with one it cuts EXACTLY square, every time, forever. Every project after this is better because you built this one. Leverage beats difficulty.

Outfeed support and a flat assembly surface. Long boards stop falling off the back of the saw, and glue-ups stop happening on the floor. It is easier than the sled — put it second anyway, because you want the sled's square cuts to build it with.

Now you have a square saw and a flat surface, so you can build something that has to be accurate. A tool cabinet is a case with a door — real casework, at a scale where a mistake costs a sheet of plywood instead of a slab of walnut.

Last, because it is the hardest thing in this list and the one you least want to get wrong. A bench must be flat, heavy, and dead still. Build it once you can already cut square, glue up flat panels, and lay out joinery accurately — which the three above teach you.
